metadata.title: Guideline on the Clinical Investigation of Medicinal Products to Prevent Development/Slow Progression of Chronic Renal Insufficiency

rules.rule_text: CKD is defined as renal damage or GFR below 60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for 3 months or more, irrespective of the cause. CKD classification is based on GFR and albuminuria levels per K/DOQI guidelines.
rules.verbatim_quote: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) - renal damage or glomerular filtration rate (GFR) below 60 ml/min per 1.73 m2 for 3 months or more, irrespective of the cause.
rules.exceptions: KDIGO or NICE modifications could be considered where relevant

rules.rule_text: Laboratory and clinical data documenting the presence of renal damage for at least 3 months are required to define CKD. Renal damage can be identified by morphological abnormalities, proteinuria/albuminuria, or GFR less than 60 ml/min/1.73 m2.
rules.verbatim_quote: For CKD, laboratory and clinical data are needed to define the presence of renal damage for at least 3 months. Renal damage could be identified based on pathological morphological abnormalities of the kidney, such as the presence of polycystic kidney disease or the presence of markers of renal damage, such as proteinuria/albuminuria, or GFR less than 60 ml/min/1.73 m2 without any other evidence of renal damage.

rules.rule_text: The study population must be representative of the target population and characterised at baseline with respect to specific disease, risk factors for CKD development or progression, co-morbidities, and co-medication.
rules.verbatim_quote: The study population should be representative of the target population and be characterised at baseline with respect to specific disease and the risk factors for the development or progression of renal insufficiency, co-morbidities and co-medication used.
rules.exceptions: May be restricted based on high risk profiling in initial studies if properly justified

rules.rule_text: Documentation of diagnostic criteria is required for both the main disease and its staging, and the risk factors known to be associated with CKD progression.
rules.verbatim_quote: Documentation of the diagnostic criteria is required both for main disease and its staging and the risk factors known to be associated with CKD progression.

rules.rule_text: Information on all concomitant medicinal and dietary products must be collected. Concomitant products that could affect results must be predefined or excluded if feasible; otherwise minimised by proper stratification.
rules.verbatim_quote: Every attempt should be made to collect information on both medicinal and relevant dietary products being taken (such as low protein diet, low salt diet, low phosphorus diet, etc.). Concomitant products and other factors that could affect the results during the study must be predefined or excluded if feasible.

rules.rule_text: Three categories of CKD risk factors must be considered: susceptibility factors (older age, family history, race), initiation factors (diabetes, high blood pressure, autoimmune disease, nephrotoxic medicines), and progression factors (high proteinuria, poor glycaemic control, nephrotoxic medicines, hypovolemia).
rules.verbatim_quote: Susceptibility factors, increasing susceptibility to renal damage, such as older age, family history, and race. Initiation factors, directly initiating renal damage, such as diabetes, high blood pressure, specific autoimmune disease, and others such as nephrotoxic medicines. Progression factors, causing worsening of renal damage and faster decline in renal function after initiation of renal damage, such as high level of proteinuria, high blood pressure, poor glycaemic control, nephrotoxic medicines or hypovolemia.
rules.exceptions: No consensus on the importance of every individual risk factor or cut-off levels

rules.rule_text: For non-specific CKD management, adequacy of treatment of the primary non-renal disease must be considered in study design, including glycaemic control, blood pressure control, ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy, statin treatment, avoidance of DHP calcium channel blockers, protein intake control, and fluid balance monitoring.
rules.verbatim_quote: for non-specific management of CKD the adequacy of treatment of the primary non-renal disease (such as hypertension, transplantation, or diabetes, as this is described in respective disease specific guidelines) is important and should be considered in the design of the study. This can include the following: Glycaemic control; Control of blood pressure; ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy; Statin treatment; Avoidance of DHP calcium channel blockers unless needed for blood pressure control; Control of protein intake; Monitoring of proper fluid balance, avoidance of dehydration

rules.rule_text: Renal biopsies are not required to set diagnosis in pivotal studies (e.g., diabetic nephropathy) as a general rule. However, biopsies should be considered when a specific treatment depends on histological diagnosis or for mechanistic/proof-of-concept studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general rule, the renal biopsy is not required if not used in general practice to set diagnosis in case of pivotal studies, e.g. diabetic nephropathy. However, it could be considered to perform renal biopsies in clinical trials where a specific treatment is dependent upon the histological diagnosis of the renal disease, such as in case of mechanistic or proof of concept studies.
rules.exceptions: Required for glomerular diseases, chronic allograft nephropathy where histological diagnosis is essential

rules.rule_text: Representativeness of the study population for the EU must be considered, and region-specific policies (e.g., renal replacement therapy timing, pre-emptive transplantation) must be addressed in trial design through stratification.
rules.verbatim_quote: Enrolment of patients in clinical studies is expected to be governed by region specific policies and practices (such as starting renal replacement therapies earlier than at CKD 5, employing different pre-emptive transplantation policies). Consideration should therefore be given on representativeness of the population for the EU and on consequences for trial design (e.g. stratification).

rules.rule_text: Two distinct therapeutic claims are supported: primary prevention (preventing CKD in at-risk populations without signs of kidney disease) and secondary prevention (slowing progression in patients with existing CKD signs).
rules.verbatim_quote: Primary prevention: This scenario describes the prevention of chronic kidney disease in a population with an increased risk (such as diabetes, or primary hypertension, etc) but without demonstrable signs of chronic kidney disease, i.e., without any sign of renal damage. Secondary prevention: This scenario encompasses the slowing of progression of chronic kidney disease in patients with existing signs of chronic kidney disease.

rules.rule_text: Claims representing only limited aspects of renal pathology (proteinuria only or small GFR changes only) are not acceptable as an independent indication. Such changes may only be reflected in Section 5.1 of the SmPC.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is not expected that additional claims representing only limited aspects of renal pathology tested, such as proteinuria only or small changes in GFR values only would be acceptable as an independent indication or constitute an independent part of the indication.

rules.rule_text: Creatinine-based eGFR estimations are preferable over cystatin C-based estimations due to better characterisation. When precise GFR determination is essential (slow expected decline, prolonged studies, high variability of non-GFR determinants), measured GFR (mGFR) is prioritised over eGFR.
rules.source_section: 5.2 Methods to assess efficacy criteria - Kidney function assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: The creatinine based eGFR estimations are preferable for the time being over cystatin C based estimations due to better characterisation of the former... when it is not reliable to estimate GFR due to great variability of non-GFR determinants of biomarkers employed for estimation it is recommended that mGFR is prioritised over eGFR.
rules.exceptions: eGFR using validated equations (MDRD, CKD-EPI) may complement mGFR for longitudinal observations

rules.rule_text: Measured GFR (mGFR) using clearance of exogenous substances (iohexol, iothalamat) should be used in a pre-specified subset of patients as a confirmatory test of eGFR in pivotal studies.
rules.source_section: 5.2 Methods to assess efficacy criteria - Kidney function assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: It is proposed to use measured GFR in a pre-specified subset of patients as a confirmatory test of the eGFR.
rules.exceptions: Difficulties in routine clinical practice usage are acknowledged

rules.rule_text: Acute hemodynamic effects on GFR decline must be considered. Study design must be justified based on the specific pattern where acute and permanent drug effects are delineated. Exploratory studies must detail: nature of acute effect, time to maximal and plateau (e.g., after 3 months), magnitude, proportionality, reversibility, and linearity.
rules.source_section: 5.2 Methods to assess efficacy criteria - Kidney function assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: The presence of acute hemodynamic effects should be considered for proper assessment of GFR decline. The study design should be justified based on the specific pattern of the acute effect where the acute effect and more permanent effect of the study drug are delineated. The patterns to be detailed during exploratory studies should include the nature of acute effect (e.g., hemodynamic), the time to maximal and plateau of the acute effect (e.g., after 3 months), magnitude, proportionality, and reversibility of the acute effect, as well as the linearity and the time needed to observe long-term beneficial verum effect.

rules.rule_text: Proteinuria must be assessed quantitatively using timed (e.g., 24-hour) urine collection or untimed spot urine for albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) or protein-to-creatinine ratio (PCR). For diurnal variation, untimed specimens must be collected on first morning samples. ACR is preferable to PCR at lower proteinuria levels.
rules.source_section: 5.2 Methods to assess efficacy criteria - Proteinuria/albuminuria assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Proteinuria should be assessed quantitatively using a timed (e.g. 24 hours) or untimed (spot) urine collection to measure albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) or protein-to-creatinine ratio (PCR). To account for diurnal variation the untimed urine specimen should be collected on first morning samples. ACR is preferable to PCR, particularly at lower levels of proteinuria
rules.exceptions: PCR may be reasonable for diabetic nephropathy; timed urine sample is the method of choice for assessing treatment efficacy

rules.rule_text: Dose-finding studies must take into account the impact of changes in PK across different CKD stages. Studies should be performed in parallel, fixed-dose design with sufficient dose levels, with controls (placebo if possible), and background therapy reflecting best standard of care.
rules.verbatim_quote: The strategy for dose-finding should take into consideration the possible impact of changes in PK in different CKD stages... Dose-finding studies should be preferably performed in a parallel, fixed-dose design, using a sufficient number of dose levels. The use of controls is recommended (placebo if possible); background therapy should be best standard of care regimen acceptable from a clinical perspective.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory trials must be designed as randomised, parallel group, double-blind studies. The goals are either superiority compared to standard of care (with or without active comparator, with or without placebo) or non-inferiority compared to an authorised active comparator.
rules.verbatim_quote: Comparative trials should be designed as randomised, parallel group, double blind studies according to the aims of product development... To demonstrate superiority compared to standard of care (may include active comparator) with or without placebo, where justified. To demonstrate non-inferior efficacy compared to an authorised active comparator

rules.rule_text: Products intended for renal damage of diverse aetiologies may require separate confirmatory studies for each aetiology.
rules.verbatim_quote: Other products would be intended for renal damage of diverse aetiologies and separate confirmatory studies might be required.

rules.rule_text: For primary prevention, the primary endpoint must be prevention or slowing of decline in GFR (measured via slope or time-to-event analyses), with or without co-primary endpoints of: prevention of proteinuria/albuminuria, time to occurrence of CKD 3, or incidence rate of CKD 3 or higher.
rules.source_section: 6.2.2 Confirmatory studies - Primary endpoints - Primary prevention
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary efficacy endpoint should be the prevention or slowing of decline in the level of renal function, defined as Clinically meaningful and stable GFR loss rate (measured either via slope or time to event analyses) with or without one of co-primary endpoints, either (a) Prevention of proteinuria/albuminuria or (b) Time to occurrence of CKD 3 or (c) Incidence rate of CKD 3 or higher
rules.exceptions: Alternatively (a) to (c) may be key secondary endpoints

rules.rule_text: For secondary prevention, the recommended primary endpoint is time to a predefined and justified loss in GFR, such as 50%. Lower magnitudes of GFR loss may be used if qualified for specific primary disease or patient populations.
rules.source_section: 6.2.2 Confirmatory studies - Primary endpoints - Secondary prevention
rules.verbatim_quote: The recommended primary endpoint is time to a predefined and justified loss in GFR, such as 50%. Other (lower) magnitudes of proportions might be used, provided this magnitude is qualified for specific primary disease or specific patient populations.
rules.exceptions: Lower magnitudes acceptable if qualified for specific disease/population (e.g., extrapolating adult data to paediatric patients)

rules.rule_text: For advanced or rapidly progressive CKD, all-cause mortality must be considered as part of the co-primary endpoint. The composite of all-cause mortality and renal failure (CKD 5 or 5D) must always be reported.
rules.source_section: 6.2.2 Confirmatory studies - Primary endpoints - Secondary prevention
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of advanced or rapidly progressive disease all-cause mortality should be considered to be included as part of the co-primary endpoint. The composite of all-cause mortality and renal failure (CKD 5 or 5D, see definitions) should always be reported.
rules.exceptions: If very low mortality event rate is expected, comparative mortality may be the main secondary endpoint

rules.rule_text: Sensitivity analyses must be planned for potential effects of differences in clinical treatment decisions on primary endpoints (e.g., timing of dialysis start), including various geographical approaches to starting renal replacement therapies.
rules.verbatim_quote: Because of potential effects of differences in clinical treatment decisions on primary endpoints, such as the start of dialysis, sensitivity analyses should be planned (e.g., encountering various geographical approaches in starting renal replacement therapies or the considerations mentioned in Section "Methods to assess efficacy").

rules.rule_text: In open trials, independent blinded reviewers must assess outcomes following pre-defined rules. A centralised blinded adjudication panel must decide on outcomes where independent reviewers lack concordance.
rules.verbatim_quote: independent blinded reviewers should assess the outcomes of an open trial and this should be performed following pre-defined rules. In cases where there is lack of concordance between independent reviewers a centralised blinded adjudication panel should decide on the outcome for such cases.

rules.rule_text: If an approved European regimen for the indication exists, comparison with that regimen is strongly recommended. In absence of approved regimen, best standard of care should be used as comparator. Placebo is a valuable option for add-on or substitutional study designs.
rules.verbatim_quote: If an approved European regimen already exists, comparison with that regimen is strongly recommended. In the absence of approved regimen for a given indication or where the standard clinical practice is use of a non-approved regimen, best standard of care should be employed. The placebo is a valuable option for or add-on to standard of care or substitutional studies, where feasible.
rules.exceptions: European scientific advice should be sought for choice of non-approved comparators prior to confirmatory studies

rules.rule_text: Subjects expected to progress require long-term observation and treatment with renoprotective products. Data from long-term studies are essential. CKD progression data following transient acute kidney injury episodes should also be collected and analysed.
rules.verbatim_quote: Subjects who are expected to have progression of renal insufficiency are required to receive long-term observation and treatment with renoprotective medicinal products. Data obtained from long-term studies are therefore essential. Where feasible, collect and analyze data on CKD progression following episodes of transient acute kidney injury.

rules.rule_text: Nephrotoxicity risks from both the investigational product and concomitant baseline therapy must be carefully evaluated, profiling magnitude and time to specific nephrotoxicity events. Investigation and qualification of new biomarkers for early safety signals (e.g., KIM-1, NGAL) is encouraged.
rules.verbatim_quote: Risks of nephrotoxicity, arising due to either the medicinal product under investigation or due to concomitant baseline therapy should be carefully evaluated profiling the magnitude and time to specific nephrotoxicity events thus enabling to assess the tolerance level and the impact on the indication claimed. It is encouraged to investigate/qualify new and existing candidate biomarkers for early safety signals, such as Kidney injury molecule 1 (KIM-1) or Neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL).

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory studies must include a sufficient number of elderly patients without upper age restrictions. Renal function in elderly must be measured using age-specific reference ranges, and optimal management of concomitant diseases (cardiovascular, diabetes, renal bone disease) is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: Confirmatory studies should include a sufficient number of elderly patients without any restrictions to the upper age limit... The accurate measurement of renal function (using age-specific reference ranges) and optimal management of concomitant diseases (e.g. cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, renal bone disease) are both important in this age group.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric development plan and timing of clinical investigation must be determined on a case-by-case basis. PK and dedicated efficacy/safety studies in children must address CKD-specific paediatric issues. Renal function should be measured using most informative estimations such as Schwartz revised composite eGFR.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetic and dedicated efficacy/safety studies in children should be undertaken to address specific paediatric issues related to development or progression of CKD... Renal function should be measured employing most informative estimations, such as Schwartz revised composite eGFR estimation (2009).
